sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after the RIGHT action. I need to compare the previous frame to the current frame to see if the player moved.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after the RIGHT action
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find the white player node in both frames
def find_player(seg):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'W':
            b = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            return (min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols))
    return None

before_player = find_player(before_seg)
after_player = find_player(after_seg)
print(f"Player before: {before_player}")
print(f"Player after: {after_player}")

# Check what changed - look at the player area in ascii
ascii_lines_before = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
ascii_lines_after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

print("\n=== Player area before ===")
for i in range(30, 35):
    print(f"{i}: {ascii_lines_before[i][18:28]}")
print("\n=== Player area after ===")
for i in range(30, 35):
    print(f"{i}: {ascii_lines_after[i][18:28]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check if anything changed at all - look at the bottom bar and other areas
ascii_lines_before = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
ascii_lines_after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Check bottom bar area
print("=== Bottom bar before ===")
for i in range(60, 64):
    print(f"{i}: {ascii_lines_before[i][10:64]}")
print("\n=== Bottom bar after ===")
for i in range(60, 64):
    print(f"{i}: {ascii_lines_after[i][10:64]}")

# Check orange/blue area
print("\n=== Orange/blue area before ===")
for i in range(44, 50):
    print(f"{i}: {ascii_lines_before[i][30:42]}")
print("\n=== Orange/blue area after ===")
for i in range(44, 50):
    print(f"{i}: {ascii_lines_after[i][30:42]}")

# Diff all lines
diffs = []
for i in range(len(ascii_lines_before)):
    if i < len(ascii_lines_after) and ascii_lines_before[i] != ascii_lines_after[i]:
        diffs.append(i)
print(f"\nLines that changed: {diffs}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see! The RIGHT action didn't move the player, but it did:
1. Move the orange/blue block to the RIGHT (from cols 34-38 to cols 38-41 area)
2. Changed the yellow bar - one Y became G (gray), suggesting the yellow bar is a timer that decreases
So the game doesn't control the white cross player directly. Instead, the actions control the orange/blue block at the bottom of the gray room! The orange+blue block moved right when I pressed RIGHT.
Let me verify this by trying other directions. The goal might be to move this block to match the blue pattern shown in the bottom-left reference box or the top target box.
Looking at the bottom-left reference (rows 53-62, cols 1-10), it has a blue "F" shape. The top target (rows 9-15, cols 33-39) has a small blue shape too.
The orange/blue block seems to be what I control. Let me try more movements to understand the mechanics better.
